breakClass: Nested collection / sub-entity projection EF Core 10 cannot translate: an inline nested `Field = (from x in db.Y join ... select new {...}).ToList()` (or a `let img = db.Y.Where(...).Select(...).ToList()` that is then projected) placed inside a SERVER-SIDE outer `select new {...}` that is enumerated by `.ToList()`/`.FirstOrDefault()` with no prior `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()` boundary. EF6 ran these (separate per-row queries / query-splitting); EF Core 10 throws `The LINQ expression ... could not be translated` / `ProjectionBindingExpression could not be translated` -> HTTP 500. Two confirmed systemic sub-classes: (A) Common.cs PDF/print builders projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections; (B) process-flow/status/service-report master grids projecting AssignType/AssignedUsers/Depts/Desgn/contacts/itemsused collections. NOTE: the HireReturn/CrossHireReturn/PackingList pdfItem builders and the MyReports/Leads/ProTask *dashboards* are SAFE because the outer source is already materialized (`from o in v` after `.ToList()`, or nested `.ToList()` sits after `.Distinct().ToList().Select(...)`), making the nested query a client-side N+1, not a 500.
why: Server-side outer query over db.SEItemss enumerated by .ToList() at ~line 8744 (no AsEnumerable boundary). The projection emits TWO nested collections per outer row: img (a let-bound db.ItemImages...ToList()) and bundle (an inline multi-join (from ay ... select new pdfBundleViewModel).ToList() which itself nests a second img=bimg collection). EF Core 10 cannot translate projecting these inline/let-bound .ToList() collections (esp. the 2-level nesting with joins) -> ProjectionBindingExpression could not be translated -> 500 on every Sales invoice PDF/print. EF6 translated it via per-row sub-queries. The codebase already proved this class 500s (tasks #5-#7 fixed GetAllSaleprofit* collection projections).
finalFix: Mirror legacy EF6 output by removing the nested collections from the server-side projection and filling them client-side via lookups (identical shape/values, no 500). Concretely in QuickNetCore\Models\Common.cs SaleData (~8664-8744): (1) Drop `let img = db.ItemImages.Where(...).Select(...).ToList()` and remove `img = img` and the entire inline `bundle = (from ay ... ).ToList()` from the EF `select new pdfItemViewModel`. Keep selecting the keys needed for lookups (b.Item is already available; project an extra `Item = b.Item` if not otherwise present). Call `.ToList()` on this scalar-only outer set first. (2) Bulk-load the item images for the materialized rows: `var itemIds = vmodel.pdfItem.Select(r => r.Id).Distinct().ToList();` then `var imgLookup = db.ItemImages.Where(im => itemIds.Contains(im.ItemID)).Select(im => new { im.ItemID, im.FileName, im.Status, im.ItemImageID }).ToList().ToLookup(x => x.ItemID);` and assign `row.img = imgLookup[row.Item].Select(x => new pdfItemImg{ FileName=x.FileName, Status=x.Status, ItemImageID=x.ItemImageID }).ToList();` (use empty list when absent to match legacy non-null shape). (3) Rebuild `bundle` client-side per row by running the same join query but materialized: for each outer row's `b.Item`, execute `(from ay in db.BundleItems join az in db.ItemBundles ... join bb in db.Items ... where az.mainItem == bItem select new {...scalars..., ay.ItemId, ay.ItemQuantity, ay.ItemUnitPrice, ...}).ToList()` (a top-level standalone query is fine â€” it materializes before projecting), then build the pdfBundleViewModel list in memory, computing ItemQuantity/ItemSubTotal/ItemTaxAmount with the captured outer `b.ItemQuantity`, and fill each bundle row's `img` from a second bulk ItemImages lookup keyed on the bundle ItemIds. Because the print path already does per-row work, a simple per-row materialized bundle query preserves the legacy profile; optionally batch by mainItem for fewer round-trips. This produces byte-identical pdfItem/img/bundle/bimg output while replacing the untranslatable nested projection with materialized client-side lookups. Apply the same transformation to the ~20 sibling sites listed in reasoning.
reasoning: I tried to refute but the evidence positively confirms the claim. (a) SERVER-SIDE: The outer query at QuickNetCore\Models\Common.cs:8664 starts `from b in db.SEItemss` (a DbSet<SEItems>, confirmed ApplicationDbContext.cs:114) and is one unbroken IQueryable<pdfItemViewModel> comprehension terminated by .ToList() at line 8744. I read the full block 8664-8744: the only .ToList() calls inside are the projected collections themselves (img @8678, bimg @8714, bundle @8743) â€” there is NO .AsEnumerable()/.ToList()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary on the outer source before the terminal .ToList(). Not a standalone scalar query, not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Genuinely server-side. (b) 500: The projection embeds correlated collection sub-queries into the server-side row â€” `img = db.ItemImages.Where(im=>im.ItemID==b.Item).Select(...).ToList()` and `bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems ... select new pdfBundleViewModel{ ... img = bimg ... }).ToList()`, the latter nesting a SECOND collection (bimg): a two-level nested collection projection over an outer query that also has DefaultIfEmpty left joins. This is the exact class the team already runtime-confirmed in this same EF Core 10 build: docs\REPORTS-AUDIT.md:152-164 documents GetAllSaleprofit* 500ing ("The query contains a projection 'â€¦DbSet<Journal>()'") and explicitly records that reducing the inner projection to a raw scalar STILL 500'd â€” proving the collection-projection shape itself, not the inner content, is what EF Core 10 cannot translate. The flagged query is the same shape but strictly worse. PORT REGRESSION: legacy EF6 at _archive\legacy-mvc5\Models\Common.cs:8665-8745 is byte-for-byte identical; EF6 ran it via per-row sub-queries, EF Core 10 throws. REACHABILITY/SEVERITY high: SaleData is the core sales-invoice PDF/print builder, called from CreditSaleController.cs (4307,4519,9027,9221,10249,10452,11743) and InvoiceTemplateController.cs:167 on every sales-invoice print/PDF; it 500s at translation time so it fails regardless of whether any images/bundles exist. View models pdfItemImg/pdfBundleViewModel are POCOs (ReportViewModel.cs:659-765), but per the documented scalar trial that is irrelevant. Note: this img-collection-in-projection pattern recurs at ~20+ sites in Common.cs (e.g. 9789,10054,10230,10442,10656,10845,11013,11343,11814,11978,12233,12983,13110,13185,13261) â€” each needs the same fix; this finding covers the SaleData instance I inspected.

EF6 ran these (separate per-row queries / query-splitting); EF Core 10 throws `The LINQ expression ... could not be translated` / `ProjectionBindingExpression could not be translated` -> HTTP 500. Two confirmed systemic sub-classes: (A) Common.cs PDF/print builders projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections; (B) process-flow/status/service-report master grids projecting AssignType/AssignedUsers/Depts/Desgn/contacts/itemsused collections. NOTE: the HireReturn/CrossHireReturn/PackingList pdfItem builders and the MyReports/Leads/ProTask *dashboards* are SAFE because the outer source is already materialized (`from o in v` after `.ToList()`, or nested `.ToList()` sits after `.Distinct().ToList().Select(...)`), making the nested query a client-side N+1, not a 500.
why: Identical server-side 2-level nested-collection projection over db.SalesOrderItems (img let-bound collection + bundle inline collection containing bimg). EF Core 10 cannot translate -> 500 on Sales Order print.
finalFix:
Mirror the legacy EF6 output by moving the nested collections out of the server-side projection (exactly the safe `from o in v` idiom already used at Common.cs:12435/12660/12859):

1. Materialize the flat SCALAR rows first (no img, no bundle) into a List:
   var rows = (from b in db.SalesOrderItems
               join c in db.Items on b.Item equals c.ItemID
               join d in db.Scaffolds on c.ItemID equals d.Item into scaffold from d in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
               join e in db.ItemUnits on b.ItemUnit equals e.ItemUnitID into punit from e in punit.DefaultIfEmpty()
               where b.SalesOrder == id && b.ItemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
               select new pdfItemViewModel {
                   ItemUnitPrice = b.ItemUnitPrice, ItemQuantity = b.ItemQuantity, ItemSubTotal = b.ItemSubTotal,
                   ItemTax = b.ItemTax, ItemNote = b.ItemNote, ItemTaxAmount = b.ItemTaxAmount,
                   ItemTotalAmount = b.ItemTotalAmount, ItemDiscount = b.ItemDiscount, ItemCode = c.ItemCode,
                   ItemName = c.ItemName, ItemUnit = e.ItemUnitName, PartNumber = c.PartNumber, PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                   CBM = d.CBM, Weight = d.Weight, ItemDescription = c.ItemDescription, KeepStock = c.KeepStock,
                   Id = b.Item   // keep the item id so we can attach images/bundle below
               }).ToList();

2. After materialization, attach img and bundle client-side per row (each db.* call now runs as its own query against the in-memory list, exactly like EF6's per-row behavior):
   foreach (var r in rows) {
       r.img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == r.Id)
                 .Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();
       r.bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
                   join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
                   join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
                   join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold from dd in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                   join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                   where az.mainItem == r.Id
                   select new pdfBundleViewModel {
                       Id = bb.ItemID, ItemUnitPrice = ay.ItemUnitPrice,
                       ItemQuantity = ay.ItemQuantity * r.ItemQuantity,
                       ItemSubTotal = ay.ItemQuantity * r.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice,
                       ItemNote = "", ItemTax = ay.ItemTax,
                       ItemTaxAmount = (ay.ItemQuantity * r.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice) * ay.ItemTax / 100,
                       ItemTotalAmount = ay.ItemTotalAmount, ItemCode = bb.ItemCode, ItemName = bb.ItemName,
                       ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName, PartNumber = bb.PartNumber, PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                       CBM = dd.CBM, Weight = dd.Weight, KeepStock = bb.KeepStock, Item = ay.ItemId, ItemDiscount = 0,
                       ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName, ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID, SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId,
                       ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic, ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription
                   }).ToList();           // bundle still materialized per row
       foreach (var bn in r.bundle)
           bn.img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == bn.Item)
                      .Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();
   }
   vmodel.pdfItem = rows;

This preserves the legacy EF6 result set exactly (the bundle quantity/amount math uses r.ItemQuantity, matching the original b.ItemQuantity) while eliminating the un-translatable 2-level nested collection projection. Apply the identical treatment to the sibling SaleData (Common.cs:8538, mobmodel/settlment inline `.ToList()`s) and the other direct-from-DbSet pdfItem builders (QuotationData @10043, WItems @10220, etc.) that share the same nested-collection shape.
reasoning:
I tried to refute this and could not. At QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs:9779 (method SalesOrderData), the assignment is `vmodel.pdfItem = (from b in db.SalesOrderItems join ... let img = db.ItemImages.Where(...).Select(...).ToList() ... select new pdfItemViewModel { img = img, bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems ... let bimg = db.ItemImages.Where(...).ToList() select new pdfBundleViewModel { img = bimg, ... }).ToList() }).ToList();`.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. I read lines 9777-9779: the FirstOrDefault() at 9777 belongs to the *previous* summary query; the pdfItem assignment is a brand-new top-level query whose outer source is the DbSet `db.SalesOrderItems`. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary anywhere between the outer `from b` and the closing `.ToList()` at 9850 â€” the entire projection (scalars + `img` collection + `bundle` collection + nested `bimg` collection) is composed into one IQueryable and translated by EF Core. Not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping; not a post-materialization client query.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” CONFIRMED. I verified the viewmodel types in QuickNetCore/ViewModel/ReportViewModel.cs: pdfItemViewModel.img is List<pdfItemImg> (line 691), pdfItemViewModel.bundle is List<pdfBundleViewModel> (696), and pdfBundleViewModel.img is List<pdfItemImg> (737). So the projection contains a 2-LEVEL nested collection: an outer collection element (pdfBundleViewModel in `bundle`) that itself carries another collection (`img`/`bimg`). EF Core 10 (10.0.9, confirmed in QuickNetCore.csproj:50) can split a single-level collection projection into multiple queries, but a collection projection whose element type contains a further inline collection projection is exactly the shape it cannot translate -> throws `The LINQ expression ... could not be translated` / ProjectionBindingExpression -> HTTP 500. This throws at query build/enumeration time, so it fails on ANY Sales Order print regardless of data -> high severity.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs:9780 is byte-identical; EF6 executed it via per-row N+1 materialization. So this is a genuine EF6->EF Core 10 port break, not a pre-existing bug.

NOTE on the one hunter inaccuracy (does not change the verdict): the hunter says "same fix as SaleData" implying SaleData was already remediated this way. In this tree SaleData (Common.cs:8538) still carries inline nested `.ToList()` projections (mobmodel @8603, settlment @8610) directly out of db.SalesEntrys, so SaleData is NOT yet fixed â€” but that premise is not load-bearing for this finding. The correct safe idiom is already proven elsewhere in the same file at lines 12435/12660/12859 (HireReturn-style): they first materialize scalar rows into a List `v` via `.ToList()`, then do `from o in v let img = db.ItemImages...ToList()` so the image/bundle lookups run client-side (LINQ-to-Objects N+1) â€” never a server-side translation failure.

why: Same server-side 2-level nested-collection projection over db.QuotationItems. EF Core 10 cannot translate -> 500 on Quotation print.
finalFix:
Mirror EF6 (separate per-row queries) by inserting an .AsEnumerable() boundary so the nested img/bundle collections are built client-side â€” this is the same idiom already used in this file at Common.cs lines 8874 and 8977.

Minimal output-preserving rewrite of QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs lines 10043-10117:

1. In the OUTER server query, drop the nested `img = img` and `bundle = (...).ToList()` members and the `let img = ...` (remove img/bundle from the projected pdfItemViewModel). Keep all scalar columns and additionally capture the keys needed for the nested lookups, e.g. project an anonymous row that includes `ItemKey = b.Item` (the value used by both `db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == b.Item)` and the bundle's `where az.mainItem == b.Item`). End the outer query with `.AsEnumerable()` (or `.ToList()`).

2. Then a client-side `.Select(o => new pdfItemViewModel { ...copy o's scalars..., img = ..., bundle = ... }).ToList()`:
   - img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == o.ItemKey).Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();
   - bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
              join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
              join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
              join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold from dd in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
              join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
              where az.mainItem == o.ItemKey
              select new { ay, bb, dd, eb }).AsEnumerable()
             .Select(x => new pdfBundleViewModel {
                 ... all the existing scalar bundle members (note ItemQuantity/ItemSubTotal/ItemTaxAmount multiply by o.ItemQuantity, preserved via captured outer qty) ...,
                 img = db.ItemImages.Where(bim => bim.ItemID == x.ay.ItemId).Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID }).ToList()
             }).ToList();

Because the bundle multiplications use the outer `b.ItemQuantity`, also capture `OuterQty = b.ItemQuantity` in step 1 and use it in the bundle math (replace `b.ItemQuantity` with `o.OuterQty`). Each subquery now runs as its own client-side query exactly like EF6's per-row behavior, and the final List<pdfItemViewModel> (with populated img + bundle + inner bundle.img) is byte-identical to the legacy output. No server-side nested-collection projection remains, so EF Core 10 translates each fragment and the 500 is eliminated.

(Note: the prompt's stated archive path _archive\legacy-mvc5 is at the repo-parent C:\Quick Soft 10-06-2026\_archive\legacy-mvc5, not under QuickNetCore\; the legacy method confirmed there.)
reasoning:
I independently read QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs around the flagged line. The method is QuotationData (starts line 9858). The flagged query is at lines 10043-10117:

  vmodel.pdfItem = (from b in db.QuotationItems
                    join c in db.Items ...
                    ... (3x DefaultIfEmpty left joins) ...
                    let img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == b.Item).Select(...).ToList()
                    where b.Quotation == id && b.ItemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
                    select new pdfItemViewModel {
                        ... scalar columns ...,
                        img = img,                         // List<pdfItemImg>  (nested collection L1)
                        bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
                                  join az in db.ItemBundles ...
                                  ... (2x DefaultIfEmpty) ...
                                  let bimg = db.ItemImages.Where(bim => bim.ItemID == ay.ItemId).Select(...).ToList()
                                  where az.mainItem == b.Item    // correlated to OUTER b.Item
                                  select new pdfBundleViewModel { ..., img = bimg }   // nested collection L2
                                 ).ToList()                  // List<pdfBundleViewModel> (nested collection L1)
                    }).ToList();

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. db.QuotationItems / db.Items / db.ItemImages / db.BundleItems / db.ItemBundles are all real EF Core DbSet<> on ApplicationDbContext (verified: ApplicationDbContext.cs lines 103,132,311,312). The query is a STANDALONE top-level query whose source is the raw DbSet (an IQueryable), not a previously materialized local. The enclosing .ToList() at line 10117 is the FIRST materialization boundary; there is NO prior .AsEnumerable()/.ToList()/.AsEnumerable boundary on the outer source. The .ToList() calls inside (img let, bimg let, bundle subquery) sit INSIDE the projection and do not materialize the outer source. It is not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping, not after a boundary, and not the SAFE "from o in v" already-materialized dashboard pattern the hunter excluded. The earlier vmodel = (...).FirstOrDefault() at line 9962 is a separate top-level query and is irrelevant to this one.

(b) WOULD 500 in EF Core 10 â€” confirmed. The outer server-side select projects TWO nested collections, and one of them (bundle) is itself a correlated subquery (where az.mainItem == b.Item) that projects ANOTHER nested collection (img = bimg). View-model types (ReportViewModel.cs 659-765) confirm: pdfItemViewModel.img = List<pdfItemImg>, pdfItemViewModel.bundle = List<pdfBundleViewModel>, pdfBundleViewModel.img = List<pdfItemImg> â€” i.e. a 2-level-deep nested-collection projection plus let-bound .ToList() correlated subqueries as collection members, over several DefaultIfEmpty left-join groups. EF Core 10 cannot translate this combination and throws "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" / ProjectionBindingExpression at enumeration -> HTTP 500 on Quotation print. EF6 ran it via separate per-row queries, so it worked in the legacy app.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed byte-for-byte. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs lines 10044-10118 are identical to the ported lines 10043-10117 (same QuotationData, same img+bundle+bimg nested projection). The EF6 original ran; the port has the exact same source, so the only difference is the provider. This is the hunter's confirmed systemic sub-class (A) (Common.cs PDF/print builders projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections).

SEVERITY: high. Every Quotation print whose quotation has items reaches this; the failure is at translation/enumeration time, independent of data values, so it is not edge-data-gated. I cannot refute the flag â€” the location, server-side status, regression status, and 500 cause all hold.

EF6 ran these (separate per-row queries / query-splitting); EF Core 10 throws `The LINQ expression ... could not be translated` / `ProjectionBindingExpression could not be translated` -> HTTP 500. Two confirmed systemic sub-classes: (A) Common.cs PDF/print builders projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections; (B) process-flow/status/service-report master grids projecting AssignType/AssignedUsers/Depts/Desgn/contacts/itemsused collections. NOTE: the HireReturn/CrossHireReturn/PackingList pdfItem builders and the MyReports/Leads/ProTask *dashboards* are SAFE because the outer source is already materialized (`from o in v` after `.ToList()`, or nested `.ToList()` sits after `.Distinct().ToList().Select(...)`), making the nested query a client-side N+1, not a 500.
why: Same server-side img+bundle nested-collection projection over db.PFItemss. EF Core 10 cannot translate -> 500 on ProForma print.
finalFix:
Mirror the legacy N+1 behavior by stripping the nested collections out of the EF projection, materializing the flat rows, then attaching img/bundle via client-side lookups. Output is preserved exactly.

1) Outer query â€” remove the `let img = ...` and replace the inline `bundle = (...).ToList()` and `img = img` with placeholders, materialize, then fill in per row:

vmodel.pdfItem = (from b in db.PFItemss
                  join c in db.Items on b.Item equals c.ItemID
                  join d in db.Scaffolds on c.ItemID equals d.Item into scaffold
                  from d in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                  join e in db.ItemUnits on b.ItemUnit equals e.ItemUnitID into punit
                  from e in punit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                  join g in db.ItemBundles on c.ItemID equals g.mainItem into bundle
                  from g in bundle.DefaultIfEmpty()
                  where b.ProForma == id && b.itemNote != "-:{Bundle_Item}"
                  select new pdfItemViewModel
                  {
                      Id = c.ItemID,
                      ItemUnitPrice = b.ItemUnitPrice,
                      ItemQuantity = b.ItemQuantity,
                      ItemSubTotal = b.ItemSubTotal,
                      ItemNote = b.itemNote,
                      ItemTax = b.ItemTax,
                      ItemTaxAmount = b.ItemTaxAmount,
                      ItemTotalAmount = b.ItemTotalAmount,
                      ItemDiscount = b.ItemDiscount,
                      ItemCode = c.ItemCode,
                      ItemName = c.ItemName,
                      ItemUnit = e.ItemUnitName,
                      PartNumber = c.PartNumber,
                      InSaleInvoice = c.InSaleInvoice,
                      PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                      CBM = d.CBM,
                      Weight = d.Weight,
                      // img attached client-side below
                      ItemDescription = c.ItemDescription,
                      KeepStock = c.KeepStock,
                      // bundle attached client-side below
                  }).ToList();   // <-- materialization boundary

// client-side fill (now N+1, exactly like EF6):
foreach (var it in vmodel.pdfItem)
{
    it.img = db.ItemImages
        .Where(im => im.ItemID == it.Id)
        .Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID })
        .ToList();

    it.bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems
                 join az in db.ItemBundles on ay.ItemBundle equals az.ItemBundleId
                 join bb in db.Items on ay.ItemId equals bb.ItemID
                 join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into scaffold
                 from dd in scaffold.DefaultIfEmpty()
                 join eb in db.ItemUnits on ay.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bpunit
                 from eb in bpunit.DefaultIfEmpty()
                 where az.mainItem == it.Id
                 select new pdfBundleViewModel
                 {
                     Id = bb.ItemID,
                     ItemUnitPrice = ay.ItemUnitPrice,
                     ItemQuantity = (ay.ItemQuantity * it.ItemQuantity),
                     ItemSubTotal = (ay.ItemQuantity * it.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice),
                     ItemNote = "",
                     ItemTax = ay.ItemTax,
                     ItemTaxAmount = ((ay.ItemQuantity * it.ItemQuantity * ay.ItemUnitPrice) * ay.ItemTax / 100),
                     ItemTotalAmount = ay.ItemTotalAmount,
                     ItemCode = bb.ItemCode,
                     ItemName = bb.ItemName,
                     ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName,
                     PartNumber = bb.PartNumber,
                     PNoStatus = PNoStatus,
                     CBM = dd.CBM,
                     Weight = dd.Weight,
                     // img attached client-side below
                     KeepStock = bb.KeepStock,
                     Item = ay.ItemId,
                     ItemDiscount = 0,
                     ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName,
                     ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID,
                     SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId,
                     ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic,
                     ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription
                 }).ToList();   // <-- materialization boundary

    foreach (var bd in it.bundle)
    {
        bd.img = db.ItemImages
            .Where(bim => bim.ItemID == bd.Item)
            .Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID })
            .ToList();
    }
}

Notes preserving exact legacy semantics:
- The original `bundle` sub-query referenced the OUTER row's `b.Item` (in `where az.mainItem == b.Item`) and `b.ItemQuantity` (in the quantity math). After moving client-side, use `it.Id` for `b.Item` (Id = c.ItemID and the join is c.ItemID == g.mainItem, and the where used b.Item which equals c.ItemID via the join) and `it.ItemQuantity` for `b.ItemQuantity`. If b.Item could differ from c.ItemID in data, capture b.Item into the outer projection (add a field) and use that captured value instead of it.Id â€” safest is to add `Item = b.Item` to pdfItemViewModel and key bundle off it.
- `img = img` originally keyed on `b.Item`; client-side keep keying on the same item id (it.Id / captured b.Item).
- Apply the identical transformation to the sibling builders in the same systemic class (PurchaseQuotationData at ~10840 and any other Common.cs PDF builders with the img+bundle+bimg shape) for consistency, per prior batches.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED. File: QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs, method ProFormaData (starts line 10926), assignment at line 11003, query terminated by .ToList() at line 11075.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” verified. The query `vmodel.pdfItem = (from b in db.PFItemss join c in db.Items ... ).ToList()` sources directly from the raw DbSet db.PFItemss and composes an IQueryable with no prior .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary; it is materialized only at the trailing .ToList() (line 11075). It is not a standalone post-materialization client query and not a group-aggregate over a non-empty IGrouping. The earlier `vmodel = (from a in db.ProFormas...).FirstOrDefault()` (lines 10929-11001) is an unrelated separate query, not the source of pdfItem.

(b) Would throw 500 in EF Core 10 â€” verified. The server-side outer `select new pdfItemViewModel{...}` inline-projects two genuine nested collections: `img = img` where `let img = db.ItemImages.Where(im=>im.ItemID==b.Item).Select(...).ToList()` (line 11013), and `bundle = (from ay in db.BundleItems ... select new pdfBundleViewModel{ ... img = bimg ... }).ToList()` (lines 11037-11074) which ITSELF contains a further-nested `let bimg = db.ItemImages.Where(...).Select(...).ToList()` (line 11046). Field types confirmed in ViewModel/ReportViewModel.cs: pdfItemViewModel.img = List<pdfItemImg> (line 691), .bundle = List<pdfBundleViewModel> (line 696), pdfBundleViewModel.img = List<pdfItemImg> (line 737). This is exactly the confirmed sub-class (A): Common.cs PDF/print builder projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections inside a server-side select that EF Core 10 cannot translate (ProjectionBindingExpression / 'could not be translated'), whereas EF6 ran these as per-row separate queries.

Port regression â€” verified. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs lines 11004-11076 is byte-for-byte identical and ran fine under EF6. It is the direct sibling of the already-fixed PurchaseQuotationData builder immediately above (lines ~10840-10909) which has the same img+bundle+bimg shape.

Severity HIGH: triggers on realistic data â€” any ProForma with line items being printed (PDF/print path) hits this; not an edge-only case.

breakClass: Nested collection / sub-entity projection EF Core 10 cannot translate: an inline nested `Field = (from x in db.Y join ... select new {...}).ToList()` (or a `let img = db.Y.Where(...).Select(...).ToList()` that is then projected) placed inside a SERVER-SIDE outer `select new {...}` that is enumerated by `.ToList()`/`.FirstOrDefault()` with no prior `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()` boundary. EF6 ran these (separate per-row queries / query-splitting); EF Core 10 throws `The LINQ expression ... could not be translated` / `ProjectionBindingExpression could not be translated` -> HTTP 500. Two confirmed systemic sub-classes: (A) Common.cs PDF/print builders projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections; (B) process-flow/status/service-report master grids projecting AssignType/AssignedUsers/Depts/Desgn/contacts/itemsused collections. NOTE: the HireReturn/CrossHireReturn/PackingList pdfItem builders and the MyReports/Leads/ProTask *dashboards* are SAFE because the outer source is already materialized (`from o in v` after `.ToList()`, or nested `.ToList()` sits after `.Distinct().ToList().Select(...)`), making the nested query a client-side N+1, not a 500.
why: Three sibling server-side branches (DummyPEItems2 @11490, PEItemss @11571, PEItemss @11653) all project the img (let-bound) + bundle (inline, with inner bimg) collections. EF Core 10 cannot translate any of them -> 500 on Purchase Entry print regardless of the StockTransferUpdate branch taken.
finalFix:
Apply the SAME materialize-then-attach fix to ALL THREE branches (Common.cs ~11490, ~11571, ~11653), mirroring the legacy EF6 result. For each branch:

1) Reduce the OUTER query to scalar columns only (drop `img = img` and the inline `bundle = (...).ToList()` from the `select new pdfItemViewModel`, and drop the `let img = ...` line), then `.ToList()` to force client-side enumeration. Keep all scalar assignments (Id, ItemUnitPrice, ItemQuantity, ItemCode, ItemName, CBM, Weight, etc.) and carry `Id = b.ItemID` plus `Item`/`ItemDiscount` so the later lookups can correlate.

2) After materializing the outer list, populate `img` per row from a client-side lookup:
   `foreach (var row in vmodel.pdfItem) row.img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == row.Id).Select(im => new pdfItemImg { FileName = im.FileName, Status = im.Status, ItemImageID = im.ItemImageID }).ToList();`

3) Populate `bundle` per row with the bundle subquery moved to top-level (now a standalone server-side query, which IS translatable), but with ITS inner `bimg` collection also materialized-then-attached the same way. Concretely, for each outer row (row.Id == b.ItemID), run:
   `var bundleRows = (from ab in db.PEItemss join bb in db.Items on ab.Item equals bb.ItemID join dd in db.Scaffolds on bb.ItemID equals dd.Item into sc from dd in sc.DefaultIfEmpty() join eb in db.ItemUnits on ab.ItemUnit equals eb.ItemUnitID into bu from eb in bu.DefaultIfEmpty() where ab.PurchaseEntry == id && ab.itemNote == "-:{Bundle_Item}" && ab.ItemDiscount == row.Id select new pdfBundleViewModel { Id = bb.ItemID, ItemUnitPrice = ab.ItemUnitPrice, ItemQuantity = ab.ItemQuantity, ItemSubTotal = ab.ItemSubTotal, ItemNote = "", ItemTax = ab.ItemTax, ItemTaxAmount = ab.ItemTaxAmount, ItemTotalAmount = ab.ItemTotalAmount, ItemCode = bb.ItemCode, ItemName = bb.ItemName, ItemUnit = eb.ItemUnitName, PartNumber = bb.PartNumber, PNoStatus = PNoStatus, CBM = dd.CBM, Weight = dd.Weight, KeepStock = bb.KeepStock, Item = ab.Item, ItemDiscount = 0, ItemWithCode = bb.ItemCode + " - " + bb.ItemName, ItemUnitID = bb.ItemUnitID, SubUnitId = bb.SubUnitId, ItemArabic = bb.ItemArabic, ItemDescription = bb.ItemDescription }).ToList();`
   Then attach each bundle row's image collection client-side:
   `foreach (var br in bundleRows) br.img = db.ItemImages.Where(bim => bim.ItemID == br.Item).Select(bim => new pdfItemImg { FileName = bim.FileName, Status = bim.Status, ItemImageID = bim.ItemImageID }).ToList();`
   `row.bundle = bundleRows;`

This preserves the exact legacy output (same rows, same img/bundle/bimg contents, same `b.ItemID == ab.ItemDiscount` correlation) while eliminating the nested-correlated-collection-in-server-projection that EF Core 10 cannot translate. The bundle correlation `b.ItemID == ab.ItemDiscount` maps to `ab.ItemDiscount == row.Id` since `row.Id = b.ItemID` in the outer projection. Note this is an N+1 query pattern (acceptable for a single-document print path, and is exactly how EF6 behaved). Apply identically to the DummyPEItems2 branch (11490) and both PEItemss branches (11571, 11653).
reasoning:

Location: QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs, method PurchaseData(long id, ...), three sibling branches at lines 11490 (db.DummyPEItems2), 11571 (db.PEItemss), and 11653 (db.PEItemss). All three are structurally identical (just different outer source), so the print 500s regardless of the StockTransferUpdate / dummyvalue branch taken.

SERVER-SIDE confirmed: The outer query `vmodel.pdfItem = (from a in db.DummyPEItems2 ... select new pdfItemViewModel {...}).ToList()` is a single IQueryable enumerated by the trailing `.ToList()` at 11567/11648/11730. db.DummyPEItems2 and db.PEItemss are DbSet<T> (verified in ApplicationDbContext.cs:126,435 and ItemImages at :103) â€” i.e. IQueryable roots. There is NO `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()` boundary anywhere before the outer `.ToList()`; the entire tree composes server-side. It is not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. The hunter's SAFE counter-examples (HireReturn/CrossHireReturn/PackingList builders, MyReports/Leads/ProTask dashboards) do NOT apply here because there is no prior materialization of the outer source.

WOULD 500 in EF Core 10: Inside the server-side `select new pdfItemViewModel` there are TWO correlated collection projections: (1) `img = img` where `let img = db.ItemImages.Where(im => im.ItemID == a.Item).Select(...).ToList()` (target property List<pdfItemImg>, confirmed ReportViewModel.cs:691), and (2) `bundle = (from ab in db.PEItemss join ... let bimg = db.ItemImages.Where(...).Select(...).ToList() ... select new pdfBundleViewModel { img = bimg, ... }).ToList()` (target List<pdfBundleViewModel> at :696, with its OWN inner correlated collection bimg -> List<pdfItemImg> at :737). This is exactly systemic sub-class (A): Common.cs PDF/print builders projecting img + bundle (+inner bimg) collections inside a server-side outer projection. EF6 (legacy at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs:11491+, which is byte-for-byte identical apart from a 1-line offset, proving a faithful port) executed these as separate per-row queries / query-splitting. EF Core 10's relational translator cannot translate a projection that materializes a `let`-bound correlated `.ToList()` collection AND a second correlated `.ToList()` subquery (deepened by the inner bimg) in the same outer projection -> throws "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" / ProjectionBindingExpression -> HTTP 500 whenever a Purchase Entry is printed and any matching DummyPEItems2/PEItemss rows exist. Severity: HIGH (fires on realistic data â€” any purchase entry with line items).

This finding is a genuine port regression and matches the documented batch 23/24-26 collection-projection class already fixed elsewhere in this codebase.

breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new projection of an unmaterialized IQueryable (from b in db.SalesOrders ... ).FirstOrDefault() at L9777. DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain CLR helper, not a registered EF function, so EF Core 10 cannot translate it inside the server-side projection -> 'could not be translated' -> 500. Legacy (archive L9757) used EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays which maps to SQL DATEADD. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Models/Common.cs, method SalesOrderData, replace the L9756 line:

    validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.SODate, (b.SOValidity == null) ? 0 : b.SOValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

with the EF-native, DATEADD-translatable form (mirrors legacy DbFunctions.AddDays semantics; SODate is non-nullable DateTime so use it directly, no .Value):

    validity = (b.SODate.AddDays((b.SOValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.SOValidity.Value + 1)) >= DateTime.Now) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Rationale: EF Core 10 translates `DateTime.AddDays(int)` on a mapped column to SQL DATEADD, so it stays server-side and preserves the legacy result. `DateTime.Now <= AddDays(x)` is logically equivalent to `AddDays(x) >= DateTime.Now`, so output is unchanged. The days argument matches legacy: 0 when SOValidity is null, else SOValidity+1. (If you prefer to keep the exact original comparison direction, `DateTime.Now <= b.SODate.AddDays(...)` also translates and works.)
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the hunter's claim. The query in SalesOrderData (Models/Common.cs L9708) is a single server-side LINQ-to-Entities composition: `vmodel = (from b in db.SalesOrders join ... select new pdfSummaryViewModel {...}).FirstOrDefault();` spanning L9711-L9777. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.ToArray()`/`.AsEnumerable()` materialization boundary anywhere between `db.SalesOrders` and the terminal `.FirstOrDefault()`, and the `validity` projection at L9756 is inside the `select new` that EF Core 10 must translate to SQL. So fragment is genuinely SERVER-SIDE.

`DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs L67-71) is a plain `public static` CLR method (`date?.AddDays(days ?? 0)`), explicitly NOT a registered EF function â€” its own comment says "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated." EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary user static method call inside a server-side projection, so the LINQ provider throws InvalidOperationException 'could not be translated' at query execution -> HTTP 500.

PORT REGRESSION confirmed by side-by-side: legacy EF6 at _archive\legacy-mvc5\Models\Common.cs L9757 uses `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.SODate, ...)` â€” the EF6 canonical function that maps to SQL DATEADD and works. The port swapped it for the untranslatable compat shim.

Entity types: Models/SalesOrder.cs â€” `SODate` is non-nullable `DateTime` (L13), `SOValidity` is `int?` (L41). This 500s on EVERY call to SalesOrderData (not edge data) because the validity column is always projected, hence severity HIGH.

NOTE: the hunter's PROPOSED fix `b.SODate.Value.AddDays(...)` is itself incorrect â€” `SODate` is a non-nullable `DateTime` and has no `.Value` member (won't compile). The verdict stands as confirmed; I supply a corrected fix below. (There are identical sibling occurrences at L9932 Quotation/QuotDate, L10818 PQuotDate, L11945 PODate that share this bug class, but they are outside the flagged location so I only flag the SalesOrder one as confirmed here.)

EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new projection of unmaterialized query enumerated by .FirstOrDefault(). CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Models/Common.cs line 10817, replace the untranslatable CLR helper with the EF-translatable instance AddDays on the non-nullable DateTime column:

validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.PQuotDate.AddDays((b.PQuotValidity == null) ? 0 : b.PQuotValidity.Value + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Notes:
- PQuotDate is non-nullable DateTime, so use `b.PQuotDate.AddDays(...)` (NOT `b.PQuotDate.Value` â€” that would not compile).
- PQuotValidity is int?; `.Value + 1` is safe because it is only reached in the non-null branch of the `== null` ternary, exactly mirroring legacy.
- DateTime.AddDays(double) translates to SQL DATEADD in EF Core 10, matching EF6's DbFunctions.AddDays.
- The `<=` comparison direction is kept identical to legacy EF6 (_archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs:10818) to preserve boundary-day output.
- The identical regression should be fixed the same way in the MyReportsController and PurchaseQuotationController copies of this `validity = ... DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.PQuotDate, ...)` expression if they are in the live tree.
reasoning:
I could not refute this; the claim holds on independent inspection.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. The flagged expression is at Models/Common.cs line 10817, inside method PurchaseQuotationData (starts line 10770). It lives in the `select new pdfSummaryViewModel { ... }` projection of the LINQ query that begins `from b in db.PurchaseQuotations ...` (line 10773) and is enumerated by `.FirstOrDefault()` at line 10832. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary anywhere between the `from` and the `FirstOrDefault()` â€” the entire projection is composed into an IQueryable and translated to SQL. It is not a standalone top-level local query and not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Genuinely server-side.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” confirmed. DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs line 70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`) is a plain CLR helper, NOT a registered/HasDbFunction EF function. EF Core 10 has no translation for an arbitrary user-defined static method appearing in a server-side projection, so query compilation throws InvalidOperationException 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500 whenever this print endpoint runs and a matching PurchaseQuotation row exists (i.e., on realistic data). High severity.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Models/Common.cs line 10818 uses `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.PQuotDate, ...)`, the EF6 canonical function that DOES translate to SQL DATEADD. The port mechanically substituted DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays (untranslatable). Same regression also exists in the legacy/ported MyReportsController and PurchaseQuotationController copies of this expression.

One correction to the hunter's proposed fix: PurchaseQuotation.PQuotDate is a NON-nullable `DateTime` (Models/PurchaseQuotation.cs line 16; also ViewModel line 19), and PQuotValidity is `int?` (line 44). So `b.PQuotDate.Value` in the hunter's fix would not compile â€” `.Value` is invalid on a non-nullable DateTime. The minimal output-preserving fix uses the instance `DateTime.AddDays(double)` (EF Core translates this to DATEADD) directly on the non-nullable column, and `.Value` only on the nullable int inside the already-null-guarded false branch.

Output-preservation note: legacy compares `DateTime.Now <= AddDays(...)` (today still within validity => Active). Preserving the original `<=` direction (rather than flipping to `>=`) keeps boundary-day behavior identical to legacy; both forms are logically equal here, but keeping `<=` is the faithful mirror.

breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new projection of unmaterialized query enumerated by .FirstOrDefault(). CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Models/Common.cs line 11944, replace the untranslatable CLR helper with EF-native DateTime.AddDays (PODate is non-nullable DateTime, so no .Value needed), keeping the original comparison orientation:

  validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.PODate.AddDays((b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.POValidity + 1).Value)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

This translates to SQL `DATEADD(day, CASE WHEN POValidity IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE POValidity + 1 END, PODate)` and preserves the exact boolean of the legacy EF6 `DbFunctions.AddDays` version (legacy Common.cs:11945), so output is identical. (`.Value` on `b.POValidity + 1` is in the else-branch where POValidity is guaranteed non-null; the int argument binds DateTime.AddDays(double) and translates cleanly.) Also apply the identical swap to the two commented-out duplicates if ever re-enabled, and audit the other DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes/AddHours call sites in unmaterialized queries for the same regression.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED port regression that throws HTTP 500 in EF Core 10.

LOCATION: C:\Quick Soft 10-06-2026\QuickNetCore\Models\Common.cs:11944, method PurchaseOrderData(long id, ...) (region "Print PurchaseOrder Details").

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The expression sits in the projection of `vmodel = (from b in db.PurchaseOrders join ... select new pdfSummaryViewModel { ... validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.PODate, (b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : b.POValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired", ... }).FirstOrDefault();` spanning lines 11893â€“11963. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.ToArray()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary anywhere in this query before the terminal `.FirstOrDefault()` at line 11963. `b` is a row of db.PurchaseOrders, not an IGrouping, and this is not a standalone pre-materialized local. The whole `select new` projection (including `validity`) must be SQL-translated by EF Core 10.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” CONFIRMED. `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain user-defined static CLR helper: Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70 `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);` (comment at line 67 literally notes "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated"). It is NOT a [DbFunction]-mapped method nor EF.Functions.*, so EF Core 10 cannot translate it inside a server-side projection and throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" â†’ unhandled â†’ HTTP 500. Translation failure occurs at query-compile time, before any rows are read, so it 500s even when the PO row exists (realistic data), not just on edge/empty data. The rest of the expression is translatable (DateTime.Nowâ†’GETDATE/SYSDATETIME; the conditional `(POValidity==null)?0:POValidity+1` translates), so DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is the sole untranslatable node. Severity: HIGH â€” every PurchaseOrder PDF/print call hits this.

PORT REGRESSION vs LEGACY â€” CONFIRMED. The legacy EF6 source is line-for-line identical in the same PurchaseOrderData method: _archive\legacy-mvc5\Models\Common.cs:11945 uses `System.Data.Entity.DbFunctions.AddDays(b.PODate, ...)`, a CANONICAL EF6 function that translates to SQL DATEADD (also confirmed in legacy PurchaseOrderController.cs:221,347). The port swapped DbFunctions.AddDays â†’ DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays, the only change, which broke translation. Same regression class as committed batches 24/26.

TYPES: PurchaseOrder.cs:17 `public DateTime PODate` (non-nullable); PurchaseOrder.cs:46 `public int? POValidity`. So `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.PODate, ...)` binds the `(DateTime?, int?)` overload via implicit widening.

The hunter's claim is fully substantiated. Its proposed flipped fix (`b.PODate.Value.AddDays(...) >= DateTime.Now`) is logically equivalent (a<=b â‡” b>=a) and output-preserving, but note PODate is non-nullable so `.Value` is unnecessary; I give a tighter fix below that keeps the original `<=` orientation to minimize semantic drift.

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: Core invoice-PDF data builder. d=SEPayments is left-joined and an unpaid/credit SalesEntry has NO SEPayment row, so d is NULL. SEPayment.SEPaidAmount is non-nullable decimal; the outer query is server-side (materialized by .FirstOrDefault()). EF Core 10 reads SQL NULL into non-nullable decimal -> 500 when printing any unpaid invoice. EF6 source identical (Common.cs:8591) and produced 0. Every invoice print hits this.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs, inside SaleData, replace the two server-side reads so the null left-joined SEPayment yields 0 (mirroring EF6 default(decimal)):

Line 8590:
  Paid = d != null ? d.SEPaidAmount : 0,
Line 8591:
  Balance = a.SEGrandTotal - (d != null ? d.SEPaidAmount : 0),

These translate to SQL CASE WHEN d IS NULL THEN 0 ... and preserve the legacy output (0 paid / full grand total balance) for unpaid/credit invoices while leaving paid invoices unchanged. No other member reads on `d` exist in this projection, so these two edits fully cover the SEPayment left-join. (Note: the same projection reads many other left-joined entities' members â€” e.g. b, c, e, g, h, p, t, k, co â€” which are the same hazard class; but the specific flagged claim concerns d.SEPaidAmount, which is confirmed.)
reasoning:
I independently confirmed all three load-bearing facts and could not refute the finding.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: confirmed. SaleData (QuickNetCore/Models/Common.cs:8538) builds one continuous query expression starting at `from a in db.SalesEntrys` (line 8542) and enumerated by `.FirstOrDefault()` at line 8663. I read the full span 8542-8663: there is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary before the `select new pdfSummaryViewModel{...}` projection. So lines 8590 (`Paid = d.SEPaidAmount`) and 8591 (`Balance = a.SEGrandTotal - d.SEPaidAmount`) translate to SQL and run server-side. This is not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping and not a standalone top-level local query. The inner `.ToList()` projections (mobmodel, settlment, pdfItem) are correlated subqueries and irrelevant to the d-read.

(b) d CAN BE NULL: confirmed. `d` comes from `join d in db.SEPayments on a.SalesEntryId equals d.SalesEntry into pay / from d in pay.DefaultIfEmpty()` (lines 8547-8548) â€” an explicit left join. An unpaid/credit SalesEntry with no SEPayment row yields d == NULL. This is the normal case for credit invoices, so severity is HIGH (not edge-only).

(c) TYPE: SEPayment.SEPaidAmount is non-nullable `decimal` (QuickNetCore/Models/SalesEntry.cs:404).

WHY IT 500s: Reading a non-nullable value-type member (`decimal SEPaidAmount`) off an optional/left-joined entity (`d`) in a server-side projection compiles to a SQL column that is NULL for unmatched rows. EF Core 10's shaper performs a non-nullable read (GetDecimal) on that NULL ordinal and throws (InvalidOperationException / null-to-non-nullable materialization). EF6's LINQ-to-Entities materializer silently produced default(decimal)=0. Hence a genuine PORT REGRESSION.

PORT REGRESSION vs LEGACY: confirmed identical. `_archive\legacy-mvc5\Models\Common.cs:8591-8592` is byte-identical (`Paid = d.SEPaidAmount,` / `Balance = a.SEGrandTotal - d.SEPaidAmount,`) and worked under EF6, producing 0 for unpaid invoices.

This matches the codebase's already-fixed class (per memory: left-join null member-access on non-nullable value columns). I could not find any materialization boundary, IGrouping, or top-level-local escape hatch that would make it safe, so the adversarial default to "refuted" does not hold.
